title: Oasis Maintenance
desc: The filters that keep the oasis drinkable need replacing. Again.

steps.text:
The oasis looks pristine from the surface — clear blue water, palm shadows, the occasional fish breaking the surface. Underneath, it's an engineering project. Filtration membranes made from woven cactus fiber line the basin, catching sand, minerals, and the occasional creature that tries to nest in the waterline.

The maintenance chief wades in waist-deep and grimaces. "Filter three is clogged. I can feel the pressure difference." She points to a section of the bank where the water moves sluggishly. "If we don't swap it today, the whole east channel backs up and the dew farm loses irrigation."

She holds up two replacement filters — heavy rolled bundles of woven fiber. "One of us dives under to pull the old filter. The other feeds the new one in from above."

options.label: Dive under — you can hold your breath
Feed from above — you're better with the tools

steps.text:
The water is cold. Desert oasis water is always cold — it comes from deep underground, bypassing the surface heat entirely. You take a breath and duck under.

Visibility is surprisingly good. The filter membrane stretches across the basin floor like a translucent carpet, held in place by stone weights. Filter three is obvious — it's bulging upward, packed with sediment, the weave dark with trapped minerals.

You unhook the stone weights one by one. The filter peels away from the basin floor, releasing a cloud of trapped silt. You can't see. You work by feel, rolling the old filter into a bundle and pushing it toward the surface.

Your lungs burn. You surface, gasp, and duck back under. The chief feeds the new filter down. You catch the edge and begin hooking it to the floor weights. The clean weave is bright white against the dark basin stone.

Three more dives to get it fully seated. By the end, you're shivering but the water is already flowing faster — clear, clean, the pressure equalized.

"Good work," the chief says, wringing out her sleeves. "Filter four is due next week. You free?"

options.label: Warm up and admire the clear water

steps.text:
You station yourself on the bank with the feeding tools — a long pole with a hook for guiding the new filter into position, and a rake for clearing debris. The chief dives under.

From above, you can see her shadow moving along the basin floor. She unhooks the old filter and pushes it up. It surfaces like a gray island — heavy with silt, stinking of mineral deposits. You rake it onto the bank.

You unroll the new filter and feed it over the edge, using the pole to guide it down to the chief's waiting hands. It's like threading a needle blindfolded — she signals with tugs on the pole. One tug: lower. Two tugs: shift left. Three rapid tugs: you're dropping it on her head.

The process takes forty minutes and the chief surfaces six times for air. Each time, she reports progress. "Four weights hooked. Two to go. The silt is worse than I thought — there's a crack in the basin floor feeding sand directly into the filter chamber."

She patches the crack with waterproof clay on her last dive. The new filter seats clean. Water clarity improves within minutes — you can see the bottom of the basin again, ten feet down, stones gleaming.

The dew farm's irrigation channel gurgles back to life. The chief marks the repair in her logbook. "One crack. Four hours. This oasis will outlive us all if we keep showing up."
